{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Which track should we take to “make this stronger”? A) Primarily sentence/paragraph-level editing to tighten prose and emphasis without changing claims or structure, or B) Substantive strengthening that reframes the thesis and contribution, reorders sections, and recalibrates novelty/validation language based on the evidence (which may soften or qualify “first” and “validates”)?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Strengthening often requires a sharper problem framing and contribution statement to clarify the thesis and gap beyond sentence-level tweaks.",
    "R1.2": "Ensuring the reasoning from ambiguity → model design → training/inference → results → contribution is coherent will make the argument more persuasive.",
    "R1.3": "The 14 F1 improvement and the claim that it “validates” the objective need to be tightly linked to the reported evidence to enhance credibility.",
    "R1.4": "The “first end-to-end model” assertion is a strong novelty claim that benefits from a clear estimate of delta versus nearest prior work.",
    "R1.5": "Reordering material (e.g., problem → approach → training/inference → results → contribution) can heighten impact and readability.",
    "R1.6": "Crisper, more assertive wording and concision directly increase perceived strength without altering technical content.",
    "RX.1": "We should transparently flag uncertainty around novelty and generality claims to avoid overstating confidence.",
    "RX.2": "Avoiding or qualifying absolute terms like “validates” and “first” prevents overclaiming while maintaining a strong tone.",
    "RX.3": "All work should stay within strengthening presentation and argumentation without inventing new methods, data, or results.",
    "RX.4": "Guarding against exaggerated claims reduces the risk of misleading readers and harming scientific discourse.",
    "RX.5": "Any strengthening should preserve traceability to the original artifact and clearly anchor assertions to cited datasets, metrics, and sections."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Which track should we take to “make this stronger”? A) Primarily sentence/paragraph-level editing to tighten prose and emphasis without changing claims or structure, or B) Substantive strengthening that reframes the thesis and contribution, reorders sections, and recalibrates novelty/validation language based on the evidence (which may soften or qualify “first” and “validates”)?

rationale.R1.1: Strengthening often requires a sharper problem framing and contribution statement to clarify the thesis and gap beyond sentence-level tweaks.
rationale.R1.2: Ensuring the reasoning from ambiguity → model design → training/inference → results → contribution is coherent will make the argument more persuasive.
rationale.R1.3: The 14 F1 improvement and the claim that it “validates” the objective need to be tightly linked to the reported evidence to enhance credibility.
rationale.R1.4: The “first end-to-end model” assertion is a strong novelty claim that benefits from a clear estimate of delta versus nearest prior work.
rationale.R1.5: Reordering material (e.g., problem → approach → training/inference → results → contribution) can heighten impact and readability.
rationale.R1.6: Crisper, more assertive wording and concision directly increase perceived strength without altering technical content.
rationale.RX.1: We should transparently flag uncertainty around novelty and generality claims to avoid overstating confidence.
rationale.RX.2: Avoiding or qualifying absolute terms like “validates” and “first” prevents overclaiming while maintaining a strong tone.
rationale.RX.3: All work should stay within strengthening presentation and argumentation without inventing new methods, data, or results.
rationale.RX.4: Guarding against exaggerated claims reduces the risk of misleading readers and harming scientific discourse.
rationale.RX.5: Any strengthening should preserve traceability to the original artifact and clearly anchor assertions to cited datasets, metrics, and sections.
